summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Hugh Sinclair's book exposing how microfinance has devolved from a poverty-reduction tool into predatory lending that exploits the poor. The original promise—that poor people are budding entrepreneurs needing only capital—proved false, with 90% of loans being consumption loans rather than business investments. Interest rates in weakly regulated countries reach 140-200%, making it impossible for borrowers to benefit. Even in controlled experiments with no loan sharking, economists found no measurable benefit from microfinance. The fundamental conflict between profitability and helping the poor means greed nearly always wins, while the feel-good narrative persists.
key_points: 90% of microfinance loans are consumption loans (TVs, services), not investment loans for entrepreneurship
Interest rates reach 140-200% in countries with weak usury laws, functioning as extortion rather than aid
Microfinance funds failed to do due diligence because exposing problems would spook investors and collapse the system
Even the rare success stories (like the Mongolian head processor) simply displaced competitors rather than growing the economy
Controlled economic experiments in ideal conditions found no benefit from microfinance
money_quotes: The promise of microfinance was this: that poor people are budding entrepreneurs, who simply don't have access to the capital required to make their dreams come true. Turns out that's pretty rare in practice.
There's an inherent conflict of interest between making profitable loans and helping the poor, and greed nearly always wins.
The feel-good message of helping the poor just seems too good to give up.
Let's face it, that kind of extortionist interest rate doesn't help anyone.
I've often said, cynically, that companies don't really want data consultants because those consultants get to see the most embarrassing stuff.

Most of the entrepreneurial efforts that the poor are likely to engage in are nothing more than microfinance arms races that do little to help the local economies but do one thing for sure, namely impose a tax on business that is taken out of the local community entirely and distributed back to the rich world.
My conclusion is that microfinance is a failure in almost all ways, and for almost all people.
key_insight: Microfinance's fundamental flaw is the irreconcilable conflict between generating returns for investors and genuinely helping poor borrowers—a tension that consistently resolves in favor of profit at the expense of the vulnerable.
